title: Methodology
description: Learn about URCity's data sources, calculation methods, and quality standards.
content:
Our Methodology

URCity combines data from official government sources with proprietary calculations to give you the most accurate picture of life in any city across the US, UK, and Canada. We currently cover 164 cities across 3 countries.

Data Sources

We use only official, verified data sources:

United States

  • Council for Community and Economic Research (C2ER) — Cost of Living Index
  • Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) — Consumer Expenditure Survey
  • Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) — Regional Price Parities
  • Zillow Research — Home values, rents, price per square foot
  • HUD Fair Market Rents — Rental affordability benchmarks
  • US Census Bureau — Population, income, education, commuting
  • IRS Statistics of Income — Migration patterns
  • National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) — Climate normals
  • Tax Foundation — State and local tax rates
  • FCC Broadband Data — Internet availability and speeds
  • EPA Smart Location Database — Walkability and transit access
  • FBI Uniform Crime Reporting — Crime statistics
  • U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) — State-level natural gas prices and electricity costs

United Kingdom

  • Office for National Statistics (ONS) — Cost of living and earnings data
  • Rightmove — Home values and rental prices
  • ONS Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings — Income data
  • HM Revenue & Customs (HMRC) — Tax rates
  • Met Office — Climate data
  • Ofcom — Broadband availability
  • Ofgem — Gas and electricity price caps
  • Water UK — Water utility costs

Canada

  • Statistics Canada — Cost of living, income, and demographic data
  • Canadian Real Estate Association (CREA) — Home values and sales data
  • Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) — Rental market data
  • Environment and Climate Change Canada — Climate data
  • Canada Revenue Agency — Tax rates
  • ISED (Innovation, Science and Economic Development) — Broadband data
  • Canada Energy Regulator — Natural gas and electricity prices

Calculation Methods

Cost of Living Index

Our cost of living indices are based on the C2ER methodology, which compares prices across six categories:

  1. Housing — Rent and home prices (weight: 30%)
  2. Utilities — Electricity, gas, water, internet (weight: 10%)
  3. Groceries — Food and household items (weight: 15%)
  4. Transportation — Gas, public transit, car insurance (weight: 15%)
  5. Healthcare — Insurance and out-of-pocket costs (weight: 10%)
  6. Miscellaneous — Entertainment, clothing, personal care (weight: 20%)

The national average is set to 100. A score of 120 means the city is 20% more expensive than average.

Heating & Cooling Cost Estimation

Heating and cooling costs are calculated using a physics-based model that accounts for:

  1. Degree Days (HDD & CDD) — Derived from each city’s summer high and winter low temperatures using a sinusoidal model. Heating Degree Days (HDD, base 65°F) measure heating demand; Cooling Degree Days (CDD, base 75°F) measure air-conditioning demand.

  2. Home Characteristics — Assumes a typical mixed-vintage home with gas heating and central AC. Heating and cooling factors are adjusted by country for insulation, home size, and system efficiency.

  3. Local Fuel & Electricity Prices — State/province-level natural gas and electricity prices from official sources:

    • US: EIA state-level residential natural gas and electricity prices
    • UK: Ofgem price cap (~£1.90/therm) and typical electricity rates
    • Canada: Provincial utility rates
    • Netherlands: Dutch energy price monitor rates

Heating formula: Monthly Cost = (Monthly HDD × 0.16 therms/HDD) × Local Gas Price

Cooling formula: Monthly Cost = (Monthly CDD × 3.5 kWh/CDD) × Local Electricity Rate

This gives location-specific estimates. For example, Anchorage has high winter heating peaks while Phoenix has high summer cooling peaks.

Remote Work Score

Our Remote Work Score (0–100) combines:

  • Internet Speed (25%) — Average download speed
  • Coworking Spaces (20%) — Number of spaces per capita
  • Cost of Living (20%) — Affordability index
  • Climate (15%) — Sunny days and mild weather
  • Quality of Life (20%) — Healthcare, safety, amenities

Home Ownership Calculator

Our True Cost of Home Ownership calculator uses standard amortization formulas plus:

  • Property taxes (based on local rates)
  • Home insurance (0.35% of home value annually)
  • HOA fees (user input)
  • Maintenance (1% of home value annually)
  • Utilities including heating & cooling (based on local climate, fuel prices, and electricity rates)

Currency & Temperature Conversion

All monetary values and temperatures on city pages can be converted on-the-fly:

  • Currency: Live exchange rates fetched from open.er-api.com/v6/latest/USD on page load, with 7-day cookie cache and hardcoded fallback rates
  • Temperature: Instant °F/°C conversion
  • Auto-detection: Uses browser timezone and language settings to pre-select appropriate defaults (US→USD+°F, UK→GBP+°C, Canada→CAD+°C)
  • Supported currencies: USD, GBP, CAD, EUR, AUD

Data Freshness

Data Type Update Frequency Last Updated
Cost of Living Quarterly June 2026
Housing (Zillow/Rightmove/CREA) Monthly May 2026
Demographics Annual 2024 ACS
Climate Historical 1991–2020 normals
Taxes Annual 2026
Exchange Rates Live (API) Real-time
Heating & Cooling Estimates Per data refresh July 2026
School Data Annual 2024–2025 school year
University Data Annual 2025 CWUR rankings

School Quality Score

The School Quality Score is a composite index (0–100) that compares each city’s K-12 school system against other cities in the same country. The calculation varies by country based on available data:

United States:

  1. Student:Teacher Ratio (40% weight) — Lower ratios indicate smaller class sizes. Each city’s ratio is ranked against all other US cities.
  2. Free/Reduced Lunch Rate (40% weight) — A lower percentage of students eligible for free or reduced-price lunch indicates lower economic disadvantage. This is inverted so a lower rate produces a higher score.
  3. School Level Diversity (20% weight) — Cities with schools at all three levels (elementary, middle, high) receive full credit.

United Kingdom:

  1. Free School Meal Eligibility (60% weight) — Lower FSM eligibility indicates lower economic disadvantage. Each city is ranked against all other UK cities.
  2. School Level Diversity (40% weight) — Cities with schools at all three phases (primary, secondary, special) receive full credit.

Canada:

  1. Top-Rated Schools (50% weight) — Number of Fraser Institute ranked schools per city. Each city is ranked against all other Canadian cities.
  2. School Level Diversity (50% weight) — Cities with schools at all three levels (elementary, middle, high) receive full credit.

Data sources:

  • United States: National Center for Education Statistics (NCES) Common Core of Data (CCD), 2024–2025 school year. Includes School Universe Survey, School Staff file, and Lunch Program Eligibility file.
  • United Kingdom: Department for Education (DfE) — Schools, pupils and their characteristics, January 2025 census. Covers England only.
  • Canada: Fraser Institute School Performance Rankings, 2023–2024.

Coverage: 157 cities across 3 countries (US: 137, UK: 10, Canada: 10) with city-level data.

University Data

University presence and ranking data is available for all 1,767+ cities across 68 countries. For each city, we determine:

  • University presence — Whether the city has at least one university
  • Total universities — Count of universities in the city
  • Top-ranked university — Name and global rank of the highest-ranked university
  • Top-500 count — Number of universities ranked in the global top 500

Data source: Center for World University Rankings (CWUR) 2025, supplemented by a comprehensive world universities database. University names are matched to URCity cities using name variations and country-level filtering.

Coverage: All 1,767+ cities. Cities without a university match do not display a university section.

Limitations

  • Cost of living indices are averages and may not reflect your specific lifestyle
  • Housing data is city-wide; neighborhoods can vary significantly
  • Tax calculations are simplified and don’t account for all deductions
  • Climate data is historical; future weather patterns may differ
  • Heating and cooling estimates are based on typical home characteristics and may vary with home size, insulation, system efficiency, and local rates
  • Exchange rates are provided by a third-party API and may have slight delays
  • University data is based on global rankings and may not reflect all local or specialized institutions

Important Disclaimer

All methodology descriptions, data sources, and calculations on this page are provided for informational purposes only. URCity and Northshore Press do not guarantee the accuracy, completeness, or timeliness of any data. This content does not constitute financial, legal, tax, or real estate advice. Always consult qualified professionals and verify data independently before making relocation or financial decisions.

Have Questions?

Contact us at data@urcity.com for questions about our methodology or data sources.
